course.title: AI for Small Business
course.subtitle: Practical AI tools that save your team hours every week — no coding required.
course.description: A 3-module, 90-minute course covering the AI tools every small business owner should have in their toolkit: Claude for writing and research, ChatGPT for customer service, and Zapier AI for workflow automation. Everything is hands-on: by the end of each module you'll have built something real.
course.instructor_bio: Denise is the founder of Sagacity Network and has been shipping AI-powered platforms since 2023. She's taught AI workshops to over 200 small-business owners across the UK and Caribbean.

modules.title: Module 1 — Getting Started With AI
modules.summary: Understand what AI is good at, what it's bad at, and how to write prompts that get useful results.

lessons.title: What AI Can and Can't Do
lessons.summary: A realistic picture of AI's strengths and limitations in a small-business context.

body.markdown: Before we touch any tools, let's set realistic expectations. AI is **extraordinary at some tasks** and **surprisingly bad at others**. Knowing the difference saves you hours of frustration.

body.title: The one-sentence rule
body.markdown: If a task would take a smart intern 10 minutes of reading to do, AI can probably do it well. If it requires judgement based on your specific business context, AI needs you to supply that context.

body.text: Three things AI is genuinely great at

body.markdown:
1. **Transforming text** — summarising, translating, rewriting in a different tone.
2. **Drafting from structure** — emails, proposals, blog outlines when you give it the bullet points.
3. **Extracting structured data** — pulling key facts out of messy documents.

body.text: Three things AI is surprisingly bad at

body.markdown:
1. **Current events** — models have a knowledge cutoff; they don't know what happened yesterday.
2. **Arithmetic at scale** — calculators are more reliable for anything beyond basic maths.
3. **Making decisions without context** — 'should I hire this person?' isn't a valid AI question on its own.
**Never** trust AI output on legal, medical, or financial matters without expert review. It sounds confident whether it's right or wrong.

lessons.title: Writing a Good Prompt
lessons.summary: The four-part prompt structure that works for 90% of tasks.

body.markdown: Most bad AI output comes from bad prompts, not bad models. Use this structure:

body.code:
Context: Who you are + what you're working on
Length: How long you want the response
Examples: 1-2 examples of the output style you want
Audience: Who will read this
Request: The specific thing you want
body.caption: The CLEAR framework

body.markdown:
**Bad prompt:** *Write me a welcome email.*

**Good prompt:** *I run a small yoga studio in Leeds. Write a 150-word welcome email for new members that's warm but not saccharine. Mention our first-class-free offer. Audience: 30-50 year old women new to yoga.*

modules.title: Module 2 — Claude for Writing and Research
modules.summary: Use Claude as your always-available junior analyst for drafts, research, and document review.

lessons.title: Setting Up Claude
lessons.summary: Account setup, projects, and the Claude Desktop app.

body.alt: A laptop displaying an AI chat interface
body.caption: You'll use the web app for most tasks; the desktop app is worth it once you're using AI daily.

body.markdown: Follow along with the video above. If you get stuck, the [Claude Help Center](https://support.claude.com) has step-by-step screenshots.

lessons.title: Projects and Knowledge
lessons.summary: Upload your business documents so Claude can answer questions in your business's voice.

body.markdown: A Project is a workspace where Claude remembers everything you upload. Think of it as a dedicated 'room' for one business function.

modules.title: Module 3 — Check Your Understanding
modules.summary: A short quiz to lock in what you've learned.

body.markdown: Three questions. You can retake as many times as you want.

questions.question: Which of these is AI genuinely great at?

options.text: Rewriting a long email in a friendlier tone

questions.explanation: AI excels at transforming text. Current events and high-stakes judgement calls are the wrong fit.

questions.question: You should always trust AI output on legal questions.
questions.explanation: Never trust AI on legal, medical, or financial matters without expert review.

questions.question: Which parts belong in a good CLEAR prompt? (Select all that apply)

options.text: An example of the output style you want

questions.explanation: Context, request, and examples are all useful. Credit cards never belong in a prompt.

feedback.correct: Nice — you're ready to start using AI for real work.
feedback.incorrect: Worth reviewing Module 1 once more before moving on.
